cataloging_subject.label: Troilus and Criseyde
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's five-book Middle English poem, as represented in CME00021 by Frederick J. Furnivall's parallel transcriptions of St John's College MS L.1, Corpus Christi College MS 61, and a composite Harley MS 1239 column with explicitly supplied passages from Additional MS 12044 and Harley MS 2392. The work is distinct from those manuscript objects, Furnivall's 1894–1895 edition and Macaulay supplement, the U-M electronic encoding, and this repository's XML artifact. The XML serializes three witness versions within each book; those versions are representations of one work, not fifteen intrinsic parts.

catalog_summary.title: Troilus and Criseyde
catalog_summary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place of composition not securely established; narrative set in Troy

titles.value: Three More Parallel Texts of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde from MS. L.1 in St. John's College, Cambridge; MS. No. 61 in Corpus Christi College, Cambridge; and the Harleian MS. 1239 in the British Museum
notes: A shortened transcription of the edition's long title. It names the parallel-text source layer, not a medieval authorial title.

agents.name: A. Rogers
notes: Furnivall identifies Rogers as a member of Cambridge University Library staff; a fuller authorized form was not verified.

scope.description: Creative retelling drawing especially on Giovanni Boccaccio's Il Filostrato
The 1894–1895 parallel-text source edition represented by CME00021
Harley MS 1239 column, including explicitly marked supplies from Additional MS 12044 and Harley MS 2392
Parallel alignment, lineation, witness notes, and edition supervision
St John's MS L.1 and Corpus Christi College MS 61 columns
St John's MS L.1 and Corpus Christi College MS 61 columns

assertion.notes: The Middle English Compendium's stencil gives c1385; the approximation is not normalized to an exact year.

notes: This does not assign a precise composition place or transfer any one witness's production region to the abstract poem.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin incipits, explicits, names, and learned quotations do not make the poem as cataloged a substantively multilingual work.
Macaulay's edition-level note quotes Old French from Benoît, but that supplement is outside the abstract work and omitted from CME00021.

form_statements.description: A five-book narrative poem predominantly in seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas (ababbcc), with embedded songs, letters, prayers, and an envoy retained as verse.

summaries.value: A five-book Middle English narrative poem recounting Troilus's love for Criseyde during the Trojan War, their union through Pandarus, Criseyde's exchange to the Greek camp and relationship with Diomede, and Troilus's death and final vision beyond earthly mutability. Chaucer transforms Boccaccio's Il Filostrato through sustained reflection on love, agency, fidelity, Fortune, providence, and authorship.

CME00021 represents Furnivall's three-column edition as sequential XML divisions: for each of the five books, St John's L.1 is version 1, Corpus MS 61 version 2, and the composite Harley 1239 column version 3. Explicit passages in version 3 derive instead from Additional MS 12044 or Harley MS 2392. The XML also omits the final thirty-five lines and explicit ending in every stream. Counts below therefore describe an incomplete encoding's witness streams, not one canonical text.

extent.description: Five intrinsic books represented through fifteen witness-version divisions. The repository XML contains 3,507 LG elements and 24,542 L elements across all three streams: St John's L.1 1,174/8,219; Corpus MS 61 1,159/8,108; composite Harley 1239 1,174/8,215. Every stream ends at Book V line 1834, omitting edition lines 1835–1869, so these are encoding metrics rather than the stanza or line count of a complete canonical text.

content_parts.label: Book I
content_parts.summary: Troilus first sees and falls in love with Criseyde; Pandarus undertakes to mediate.
notes: Metrics aggregate the three encoded streams.
The composite Harley stream supplies lines 379–630 and 904–973 from Additional MS 12044 and lines 890–896 from Harley MS 2392.

content_parts.label: Book II
content_parts.summary: Pandarus advances the courtship and brings Troilus and Criseyde into communication.
notes: Metrics aggregate the three encoded streams; the shared page 40 contains the Book I/II transition.

content_parts.label: Book III
content_parts.summary: The lovers are united and experience the poem's central period of joy.
notes: Metrics aggregate the three encoded streams.
The composite Harley stream supplies lines 953–959 and 1387–1393 from Additional MS 12044.

content_parts.label: Book IV
content_parts.summary: Criseyde is exchanged for Antenor, separating the lovers and provoking debate over destiny and free will.
notes: Metrics aggregate the three encoded streams.
The composite Harley stream supplies lines 953–1085 and 1149–1155 from Additional MS 12044.

content_parts.label: Book V
content_parts.summary: Criseyde remains with the Greeks and Diomede; Troilus dies in battle and looks back on earthly love from the heavens.
notes: Metrics aggregate the three encoded streams only through line 1834.
Printed pp. 295–296, stanzas 263–267 and lines 1835–1869, including explicit endings, are absent from all three XML versions.
The composite Harley stream supplies lines 225–231 from Additional MS 12044.

related_works.title: Il Filostrato
notes: Boccaccio is Chaucer's principal narrative source, but the poem is a substantial creative transformation rather than a line-for-line translation.

evidence.locator: Title page; Books I–V; fifteen DIV3 witness versions; supplied-passage notes; final encoded line
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00021, Furnivall's source edition, Chaucer, five books, three version streams, Middle English verse, plot and thematic content, supplied passages, and an endpoint at Book V line 1834.

evidence.locator: DIV2, DIV3, LG, L, PB, MILESTONE, and NOTE counts by book and witness version
evidence.summary: Counts 5 book divisions, 15 witness-version divisions, 3,507 verse groups, and 24,542 verse lines; verifies per-book and per-stream totals and the common final encoded line 1834.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, 'Three more parallel texts of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde,' CME item CME00021.
evidence.summary: Confirms the item title, Chaucer attribution, publication data, stable identifier, and public electronic text.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium, Bibliography entry BIB625, 'Troilus and Criseyde.'
evidence.locator: Entry info; Furnivall edition; witness stencils
evidence.summary: Supplies IMEV/DIMEV identifiers, c. 1385 work dating, the 1894–1895 Furnivall citation, and manuscript designations without transferring witness-specific dates or localizations to the work.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Three More Parallel Texts of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde (Chaucer Society, First Series 87–88, 1894–1895), Google Books U8IzAQAAMAAJ.
evidence.locator: Complete bound scan; title and responsibility leaves; copying note; Books I–V, pp. 1–296
evidence.summary: Confirms the long edition title, Furnivall's editing and Harley transcription, Rogers's St John's and Corpus transcription/collation, Macaulay's separate supplement, parallel layout, supplied sources, printed book ranges, and the complete final thirty-five lines absent from XML.

evidence.citation: University of Cambridge Faculty of English, The Rhyme Royal Stanza in Troilus and Criseyde.
evidence.summary: Describes the poem's characteristic seven-line rhyme-royal stanza and rhyme scheme.

evidence.citation: Broadview Press, Troilus and Criseyde, edition description.
evidence.summary: Supports the c. 1385 context, English literary setting, Boccaccio adaptation, five-book plot, and combined romance/tragedy classification.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME00021.
evidence.summary: Provides evidence-backed separation of the work, edition, digital encoding, repository artifact, five manuscript sources, facsimiles, direct supplied-passage relations, access, and rights.

open_questions.question: Can the composition and revision chronology be narrowed beyond conventional c. 1385 dating without overstating disputed internal and historical evidence?
next_steps: Consult a current critical introduction devoted to the poem's dating and revision phases before adding normalized bounds.

open_questions.question: Which linguistic features belong to Chaucer's compositional language and which belong to the five scribal sources or Furnivall's transcription conventions?
next_steps: Use reading-level manuscript and editorial analysis; do not assign the work one source witness's localization.

open_questions.question: Should work-level counts remain tied to the incomplete XML or be supplemented with independently transcribed metrics for edition lines 1835–1869?
next_steps: Keep the current extent explicitly labeled as encoding metrics.
If the XML is repaired, recompute all Book V and total metrics before changing content_structure_status.

notes: The work record deliberately catalogs one poem, while the lineage record preserves three principal edition columns, two direct supply witnesses, and their material gaps.
Furnivall's printed arrangement is parallel; the XML's sequential DIV3 structure is an encoding transformation and must not be interpreted as intrinsic order.

George Campbell Macaulay's note on Chaucer's borrowings from Benoît de Sainte-More is edition-level supplementary scholarship, not a responsibility statement for the poem itself, and the XML omits it.
No first-known-attestation statement is inferred from Corpus MS 61; an earlier securely dated witness exists outside this immediate lineage.
No single dialect region is assigned to the abstract poem from this multi-witness source.
